items.title: Kellgren-Lawrence classification
items.contentHtml:
Kellgren-Lawrence Classification

  • Grade 0: no radiographic changes.
  • Grade 1: doubtful narrowing of the joint space and possible osteophytosis.
  • Grade 2: defined osteophytes and mild narrowing of the joint space.
  • Grade 3: narrowing moderate joint space, multiple osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis and bone deformity.
  • Grade 4: severe narrowing of the joint space, large osteophytes, marked sclerosis and severe bone deformity.
  • It is mainly used in epidemiological studies and to decide surgery; does not correlate perfectly with symptoms.

items.title: ACR criteria for hip osteoarthritis
items.contentHtml:
ACR criteria for osteoarthritis of the hip

  • Combination of hip pain plus at least 2 of 3 findings: erythrocyte sedimentation rate <20 mm/h, radiographic osteophytes (femoral or acetabular) and joint space narrowing in radiography.
  • Clinical alternative: hip pain and internal rotation <15° and morning stiffness ≤60 minutes.
  • Sensitivity of 89% and specificity of 91% for the clinical-radiological combination.

items.title: ACR criteria for knee osteoarthritis
items.contentHtml:
ACR criteria for knee osteoarthritis

  • Clinical: knee pain plus at least 3 out of 6: age >50 years, morning stiffness <30 minutes, crepitus, bone tenderness, bone enlargement, absence of warmth to the touch.
  • Clinical-radiological: knee pain plus radiographic osteophytes more at least 1 of 3: age >50 years, crepitus, morning stiffness <30 minutes.
  • Sensitivity of 91% and specificity of 86% for the clinical-radiological combination.

items.title: First line treatment: paracetamol
items.contentHtml:
First-line treatment: paracetamol

  • Paracetamol is the first-line analgesic for osteoarthritis, according to guidelines from the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) and Osteoarthritis Research Society International (OARSI).
  • Maximum dose: 4 grams per day in adults without liver disease; Reduce to 2-3 g/day if there are liver risk factors or alcohol consumption.
  • Modest efficacy, but safety profile superior to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs in the long term.
  • Does not have significant anti-inflammatory effect; useful for mild to moderate pain.

items.title: Indication for total hip arthroplasty
items.contentHtml:
Indication for total hip arthroplasty

  • Main indications: severe pain that interferes with daily activities and failure of conservative treatment (pharmacological, physiotherapy, weight loss) for at least 3-6 months.
  • X-ray should show advanced joint space narrowing (grade 3-4 of Kellgren-Lawrence).
  • Night pain and prolonged morning stiffness are predictors of good surgical response.
  • Relative contraindications: active infection, severe uncontrolled comorbidity, morbid obesity (body mass index >40).

items.title: Indication for total knee arthroplasty
items.contentHtml:
Indication for total knee arthroplasty

  • Indications: disabling pain that does not respond to conservative treatment (analgesics, physiotherapy, intra-articular injections) for ≥6 months.
  • Radiographic findings: loss of joint space in the medial or lateral compartment (grade 3-4 of Kellgren-Lawrence).
  • Nocturnal pain, morning stiffness >30 minutes and varus/valgus deformity >10° are factors that support the indication.
  • Contraindications: active infection, severe peripheral vascular insufficiency, knee extensor deficit.

items.title: Risk factors: age and obesity
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors: age and obesity

  • Age is the strongest risk factor: prevalence increases exponentially after age 50.
  • Obesity (body mass index ≥30) increases the risk of knee osteoarthritis 4-5 times; in the hip the risk is lower but significant.
  • Excess adiposity also contributes through systemic inflammatory mechanisms (adipokines).
  • Every 5 kg of weight gain increases the risk of knee osteoarthritis by 36%.
  • Weight loss ≥5% of body weight improves symptoms and delays progression.

items.foco: Sign of crepitus in the knee
items.title: Sign of crepitus in the knee
items.contentHtml:
Sign of crepitus in the knee

  • Crepitation is the sensation or sound of clicking, cracking or rubbing during active or passive mobilization of the knee.
  • It is produced by friction between irregular articular surfaces, osteophytes or intra-articular loose bodies.
  • It is a common finding in knee osteoarthritis, but non-specific (it can occur in knees asymptomatic).
  • In the ACR criteria for knee osteoarthritis, crepitation is one of the 6 clinical criteria.
  • It does not directly correlate with radiographic severity or pain.

items.title: Joint effusion in osteoarthritis
items.contentHtml:
Joint effusion in osteoarthritis

  • Joint effusion in osteoarthritis is usually mild to moderate, mechanical type (clear, yellowish fluid, with leukocyte count <2000 cells/mm³ and mononuclear predominance).
  • The presence of effusion indicates secondary synovial inflammation (reactive synovitis).
  • In knee, is evaluated by the key sign (ballotement patellar) or the bulge sign for small effusions.
  • Joint effusion in osteoarthritis is less inflammatory than in rheumatoid arthritis or gout.
  • Joint puncture is indicated if infection or crystals (microcrystalline arthritis) are suspected.

items.title: Use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
items.contentHtml:
Use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs

  • Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are second line after paracetamol, indicated for moderate to severe pain or when there are inflammatory signs.
  • It is recommended to use the lowest effective dose and for the shortest possible time to avoid gastrointestinal, renal and cardiovascular toxicity.
  • In patients with gastrointestinal risk, add proton pump inhibitor or use a selective cyclooxygenase-2 inhibitor (coxib).
  • Contraindications: chronic renal failure, heart failure, active peptic ulcer disease, allergy to NSAIDs.

items.title: Intra-articular injection of corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Intra-articular injection of corticosteroids

  • Indicated for rapid pain relief in moderate to severe osteoarthritis, especially when there is joint effusion or synovitis.
  • The effect usually lasts 2 to 4 weeks; It is not recommended to repeat more than 3-4 times a year in the same joint to avoid cartilage damage.
  • Common preparations: methylprednisolone acetate (40-80 mg in the knee) or triamcinolone.
  • Contraindications: local or systemic infection, severe joint instability, intra-articular fracture, allergy to corticosteroid.
  • The response to corticosteroid. It has diagnostic value: if there is no improvement, reconsider the diagnosis.

items.title: Viscosupplementation with hyaluronic acid
items.contentHtml:
Viscosupplementation with hyaluronic acid

  • It consists of the intra-articular injection of hyaluronic acid to restore the viscosity of the synovial fluid and provide a cushioning effect.
  • Indicated in mild to moderate knee osteoarthritis that does not respond to conservative treatment, as an alternative to surgery.
  • The clinical benefit is modest and variable; Guidelines such as those of the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) give a conditional recommendation against its routine use.
  • 1 to 3 weekly doses are administered depending on the formulation (molecular weight).
  • Contraindications: allergy to poultry products (if of chicken origin), joint infection, significant joint effusion.
